factual-accuracy.requirement: States individualism scores: South Korea collectivist (IDV 18±5), Finland individualist (IDV 63±5), Mexico collectivist (IDV 30±5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states IDV 18 for South Korea, IDV 63 for Finland, and IDV 30 for Mexico in the cultural context sections, matching the required collectivist/individualist classifications and falling within the ±5 ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States power distance scores: South Korea mid-high (PDI 60±10), Finland low (PDI 33±5), Mexico very high (PDI 81±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states PDI values for all three countries: South Korea PDI 60 (moderate-high), Finland PDI 33 (very low), and Mexico PDI 81 (very high), matching the requested score ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States uncertainty avoidance: South Korea very high (UAI 85±10), Finland moderate (UAI 59±10), Mexico high (UAI 82±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states UAI values for South Korea (UAI 85), Finland (UAI 59), and Mexico (UAI 82) in the cultural context sections. These match the required “very high/moderate/high” characterizations and fall within the specified ±10 ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Classting as teacher-moderated or teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Classting as having a “Private Counseling” feature and references anonymous “ask the teacher” options, framing it as teacher-centered. However, it does not clearly describe Classting as a teacher-moderated/teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls; those specific Classting moderation/channel details are not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Wilma has 95-98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools and handles attendance/grades/messaging
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions Wilma “dominates at 98% of schools” and discusses Wilma integration and messaging patterns, but it does not state that Wilma has 95–98% coverage specifically in Finnish basic education schools. It also does not explicitly say Wilma handles attendance, grades, and messaging together.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Google Classroom Mexico/Latin America deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) with private comments and teacher-controlled posting emphasized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Mexico/Latin America Google Classroom deployments at large scale (e.g., Jalisco ~1.3M students; Baja California 162,000) and repeatedly emphasizes private comments and teacher-controlled Stream posting permissions as core deployment practices.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or improves final grades significantly versus identified review
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses anonymous/double-blind peer review as a face-saving mechanism and mentions higher-order feedback incidence targets, but it does not state that anonymous peer assessment increases higher-order feedback or significantly improves final grades compared with identified review. No comparative outcome evidence (cognitive feedback or grades) between anonymous vs identified review is provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous question queues or LAQQ reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response repeatedly argues that private/anonymous channels reduce anxiety and embarrassment and increase willingness to ask questions, e.g., stating private comments “reduce student anxiety” and make students “more likely to engage digitally than in person,” and it recommends anonymous “ask the teacher” options and double-blind peer review to support participation versus public posting.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains that high power distance reduces willingness to publicly challenge peers or request help in digital learning environments
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response links high power distance to reduced public help-seeking and reluctance to speak up, noting students in high-PDI contexts are less likely to ask questions or express confusion in public settings and emphasizing private channels to remove the public visibility barrier.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends multiple anonymity and teacher-moderation patterns for Korea, including double-blind anonymous peer review, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous “ask the teacher” channels, and teacher-controlled/teacher-moderated posting permissions and muting within group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Finland-specific workspace features including “named edit histories,” “real-time co-editing with presence indicators,” and multiple forms of “student-initiated project groups” and student agency/self-directed collaboration in the Finland rollout sections.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends private-first design, mobile/offline editing, teacher-controlled visibility, or teacher-only posting initially for Mexican group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico-specific teacher-controlled visibility and teacher-only posting initially (Stage 1 “teacher-only access initially” and Stream set to “Only teachers can post or comment”), and it also recommends mobile-first plus offline-capable student/teacher tools for Mexican deployments.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends double-blind or anonymous peer review with rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts for Korea
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends “structured peer review with double-blind anonymity” for South Korea and repeatedly specifies “rubric-based feedback templates” and “structured sentence starters” as part of the peer review workflow, which matches the criterion’s double-blind/anonymous and rubric-guided structured prompts requirement for Korea.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named peer review with feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique for Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: In the Finland section, the response recommends “named edit histories” and “identified, transparent communication,” and it includes peer feedback scaffolding such as optional rubric scaffolding, phenomenon-based learning templates, and student participation in rubric creation—i.e., process-focused critique with exemplars/scaffolds in a named peer review context.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends two-tier identity (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or private-first peer review with structured stems for Mexico
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico use double-blind anonymous peer review with teacher visibility plus structured sentence starters/exemplar scaffolding, and emphasizes private comments as a private-first channel. This matches a two-tier identity/private-first peer review approach with structured stems for Mexico.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Claims Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, or claims Mexico has lower power distance than Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Finland has very low power distance (PDI 33), South Korea moderate-high (PDI 60), and Mexico very high (PDI 81). It does not claim Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, nor that Mexico has lower power distance than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Predicts anonymity or power-distance interventions will show stronger effects in high-PDI markets (Korea/Mexico) versus low-PDI (Finland)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly frames high-PDI contexts (Mexico, Korea) as needing anonymity and stronger teacher-controlled moderation (e.g., double-blind anonymous peer review, restrictive posting permissions) while describing Finland (low PDI) as suited to more open, identified, autonomy-oriented interaction, implying stronger effects of these interventions in high-PDI markets.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes temporal rollout strategies (private-first then gradual opening) with triggers or norms that enable stage transitions
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lays out explicit multi-stage rollout plans (Stage 1–4) for each market, describing private-first/teacher-only defaults followed by gradual opening of collaboration features. It also specifies concrete advancement triggers/norms (e.g., adoption rates, interaction frequency, moderation intervention rates, stability thresholds) that govern transitions between stages.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies negative consequences of recommended patterns (e.g., fairness concerns) and proposes compensating design elements or safeguards
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response flags multiple negative consequences (e.g., embarrassment/face-saving issues, bullying risk, surveillance/control tendencies, privacy/GDPR compliance risks, equity/digital divide disparities) and pairs them with safeguards such as private comments, anonymous/double-blind peer review, teacher-controlled posting permissions, targeted muting without public shaming, phased rollouts with moderation thresholds, and equity verification triggers.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (higher-order comments, revision adoption rates) with expected effect ranges from research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response defines measurable outcomes beyond raw counts, such as higher-order feedback incidence percentages, peer interaction frequency per week, moderation intervention rates, and revision/feedback adoption proxies. However, it does not provide expected effect ranges grounded in research (e.g., literature-based percentage-point lifts attributable to anonymity or moderation), instead presenting internally proposed thresholds and triggers.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects technical constraints (offline capability, bandwidth limits) to required collaboration design patterns in specific markets
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly ties bandwidth/offline constraints to collaboration patterns, especially for Mexico (offline-capable student tools, text-only WhatsApp notifications, data caps like <100MB/month, graceful degradation) and Finland (offline mode for rural schools). It links these constraints to specific design choices for collaboration and rollout stages in those markets.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Draws explicit connections between existing platform capabilities (private channels, moderation toggles) and cultural requirements they address
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links platform features like Google Classroom private comments, stream permission levels, and individual muting to cultural needs such as face-saving, high power distance, and uncertainty avoidance. It repeatedly frames these as culturally adaptive moderation/privacy mechanisms (e.g., “foundational feature for high PDI,” “no public shaming,” “gradual release”).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes contexts where cultural predictions don't hold or explicitly flags inferences versus direct empirical evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly distinguishes cultural inferences from documented platform behavior by citing specific deployment/training documents (e.g., UNAM manual, Baja California case study) versus broader Hofstede-based claims. It also flags uncertainty/limits implicitly by noting gaps like “No Native Anonymous Peer Review” in Google Classroom and recommending third-party tools rather than asserting universal cultural effects.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides identical design recommendations for all three markets without adapting interaction patterns to their different PDI or collectivism profiles
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations by market, explicitly tailoring moderation, anonymity, and rollout sequencing to South Korea (e.g., double-blind peer review, PIPA), Finland (teacher autonomy, identified transparency, Wilma integration), and Mexico (teacher-first, restrictive defaults, private comments). It does not give identical interaction patterns across all three contexts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis by feature or market with consistent comparative structure (e.g., Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns per feature)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into repeated country-specific sections (South Korea, Finland, Mexico) using the same phased rollout/stage headings and comparable feature categories (privacy, moderation, peer review, real-time collaboration) with parallel metrics and triggers, enabling consistent cross-market comparison.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise UX/product design terminology (interaction patterns, moderation controls, engagement metrics, feature toggles)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses UX/product terminology throughout, including interaction patterns (presence indicators, @mentions, turn-taking), moderation controls (three-tier posting permissions, muting, teacher-only posting), engagement metrics (adoption rates, interaction frequency, completion rates), and feature toggles/flags (opt-in anonymization, teacher-enabled real-time co-editing, staged rollouts).

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective, research-oriented tone connecting design recommendations to empirical findings without marketing claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a largely academic, research-report style and repeatedly ties design choices (e.g., private comments, posting permissions, anonymity) to cited documentation and cultural-dimensions research. It avoids overt promotional language and frames claims in terms of deployments, training practices, and measured/target metrics rather than marketing benefits.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents engagement effects with specific metrics or ranges (e.g., percentage increases, effect sizes) rather than only qualitative descriptions
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple quantitative metrics and ranges tied to engagement and interaction, such as adoption rates (e.g., 3% daily teacher engagement), participation thresholds (e.g., ≥50% peer feedback), interaction frequency targets (e.g., ≥3 peer interactions/student/week), and effect sizes (ηp² values). These go beyond qualitative descriptions by specifying measurable values.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same cultural insight or design recommendation multiple times using different wording without adding new analysis
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reiterates the same core recommendations—use private comments for face-saving in high power-distance contexts and use teacher-controlled posting permissions for gradual release—across the executive summary, Parts I/III, and the conclusion with only minor rewording. These sections restate the same cultural insight without materially adding new analysis each time.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Hofstede cultural dimensions framework for individualism and power distance scores across the three countries
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides PDI and IDV numbers for South Korea (PDI 60, IDV 18), Finland (PDI 33, IDV 63), and Mexico (PDI 81, IDV 30), but it does not cite or reference Hofstede’s cultural dimensions framework as the source. There is no Hofstede citation/link or explicit attribution for these scores.

citation-quality.requirement: References official documentation or deployment reports for all 3 platforms: Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom deployments
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official Google Classroom documentation (e.g., Google Classroom Help and Google for Education case studies) and includes Wilma deployment references (e.g., an academic article and an AWS scaling case report). However, it does not provide any official documentation or deployment report sources for Classting; Classting is mentioned but not backed by official references.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 academic sources on anonymous peer assessment, peer review, or participation mechanisms in educational contexts
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources relevant to peer feedback/participation mechanisms, including Dai et al. (2022) on power distance and communication (PMC) and peer-reviewed studies on Google Classroom feedback practices (e.g., Faro Educativo/Ibero report; SciELO Argentina article; Colegio de Bachilleres case study). This exceeds the requirement of at least two academic sources.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes K-12 or secondary education research on cultural differences, engagement patterns, or digital collaboration
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly focuses on secondary/K-12 contexts (e.g., “public secondary schools,” “upper-secondary education,” and staged rollouts for schools) and discusses cultural differences (power distance, collectivism), peer feedback, teacher authority/moderation, and collaboration features (anonymous/double-blind peer review, private comments, posting permissions) with engagement/interaction/completion metrics.

citation-quality.requirement: Uses Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness
citation-quality.explanation: The response relies on official Google Classroom Help/Community pages, institutional manuals (e.g., UNAM), case studies, and academic/industry reports. It does not cite Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness.
